title: A randomised controlled trial of a brief online mindfulness-based intervention
headline: A randomised controlled trial of a brief online mindfulness-based intervention
summary: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness / meditation examined brief & app-based practice. Participation in the intervention was associated with significant improvements in all measured domains, where no significant changes on these measures were found for the control group. This provides evidence in support of the feasibility and effectiveness of shorter self-guided mindfulness-based interventions.
keyFindings: There is growing evidence that mindfulness has positive consequences for both psychological and physical health in both clinical and non-clinical populations.
Participation in the intervention was associated with significant improvements in all measured domains, where no significant changes on these measures were found for the control group.
This provides evidence in support of the feasibility and effectiveness of shorter self-guided mindfulness-based interventions.
limitations: This provides evidence in support of the feasibility and effectiveness of shorter self-guided mindfulness-based interventions.
The limitations and implications of this study for clinical practice are discussed.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (mindfulness / meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: A randomised controlled trial of a brief online mindfulness-based intervention
aiSnippet: RCT, Behaviour research and therapy (2013). There is growing evidence that mindfulness has positive consequences for both psychological and physical health in both clinical and non-clinical populations. Participation in the intervention was associated with significant improvements in all measured domains, where no significant changes on these measures were found for the control group. This provides evidence in support of the feasibility and effectiveness of shorter self-guided mindfulness-based interventions.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
